name: 時事英語パック vol.1
description: 環境・テクノロジー・経済の最前線 25題

texts.title: Climate Summit Debate
texts.titleJa: 気候サミットの議論
texts.text:
A: World leaders gathered this week for the annual climate summit. Were you following it?
B: Yes. The main focus was reducing carbon emissions by fifty percent before 2035.
A: Several major economies pledged new targets, but critics say the commitments aren't binding enough.
B: Without enforcement mechanisms, these pledges remain largely symbolic.
A: Still, public pressure is pushing governments to act more decisively than before.
B: And consumer behavior is shifting too — more people are choosing sustainable companies and products.

texts.title: Renewable Energy Boom
texts.titleJa: 再生可能エネルギーの急成長
texts.text: Solar and wind energy capacity worldwide has surpassed expectations, with global renewable installations hitting a record high for the third consecutive year. According to the International Energy Agency, solar power now accounts for over fifteen percent of global electricity generation. The rapid decline in solar panel costs — down over ninety percent in the past decade — has made clean energy economically competitive with fossil fuels in most regions. However, experts warn that the pace of the energy transition must accelerate significantly to meet Paris Agreement targets. The biggest challenge now lies in upgrading aging power grids and developing efficient energy storage solutions.

texts.title: Plastic Pollution Crisis
texts.titleJa: 海洋プラスチック汚染
texts.text:
A: I read that the plastic waste patch in the Pacific is now three times the size of France.
B: That's alarming. What's being done about it?
A: Several organizations are using specially designed vessels to collect the debris. But it's a drop in the ocean, so to speak.
B: The real solution has to come from the source — reducing plastic production at the manufacturing level.
A: Some countries have banned single-use plastics, and the results have been encouraging.
B: We all need to be more conscious consumers. Every little action adds up when multiplied by billions of people.

texts.title: Electric Vehicle Adoption
texts.titleJa: 電気自動車の普及
texts.text:
A: Have you thought about switching to an electric vehicle?
B: I have, but the upfront cost is still quite high compared to a regular car.
A: True, but you save significantly on fuel over time. And government subsidies are available.
B: Charging infrastructure is my other concern. I often drive long distances.
A: The network is expanding rapidly. Most highways now have fast chargers every fifty kilometers.
B: If that's the case, I might seriously consider it at my next upgrade.

texts.title: Carbon Neutrality Goals
texts.titleJa: カーボンニュートラル目標
texts.text:
A: Japan has committed to carbon neutrality by 2050. Is it achievable?
B: Ambitious but not impossible. The key is aggressively investing in renewables and phasing out coal.
A: Nuclear energy seems to be back on the table, which is controversial given the Fukushima legacy.
B: Some scientists argue that next-generation nuclear is essential for reliable baseload power without carbon emissions.
A: Whatever the approach, the transition needs massive investment and cross-sector policy coordination.
B: And it can't just be governments — businesses and individuals need to be part of the solution too.

texts.title: Earthquake Disaster Response
texts.titleJa: 地震・災害対応
texts.text: Emergency response teams are on the ground following a magnitude six-point-eight earthquake that struck the coastal region late Tuesday evening. At least forty-seven people have been confirmed dead, with hundreds injured and thousands displaced. Rescue operations are ongoing, with crews working around the clock to reach survivors trapped beneath rubble. The government has declared a state of emergency and mobilized military units to assist with logistics and temporary shelter. Aid organizations are urging donors to contribute, noting that food, clean water, and medical supplies are in critical demand. Local hospitals are overwhelmed, and additional medical staff are being flown in from neighboring regions.

texts.title: Ocean Conservation Victory
texts.titleJa: 海洋保護区の設立
texts.text:
A: Did you hear about the new marine protected area established in the South Pacific?
B: Yes — one of the largest in the world, covering nearly a million square kilometers.
A: It's a huge win for conservationists. Overfishing and deep-sea mining have devastated ecosystems.
B: And healthy oceans are critical not just for biodiversity — they absorb about a third of global CO₂ emissions.
A: I didn't realize oceans played such a big role in climate regulation.
B: They do. That's why marine conservation and climate policy really need to go hand in hand.

texts.title: Urban Green Spaces
texts.titleJa: 都市の緑化
texts.text:
A: More cities are investing in urban forests and green corridors. Have you noticed changes here?
B: Yes — they've planted thousands of trees along boulevards and added rooftop gardens on public buildings.
A: It's not just aesthetic. It reduces urban heat islands and improves air quality measurably.
B: And the mental health benefits are significant. Research shows green spaces reduce stress and anxiety.
A: I hope the trend continues. We need cities that are livable, not just functional.
B: Urban planning needs to prioritize human wellbeing alongside economic development.

texts.title: AI in the Workplace
texts.titleJa: 職場でのAI活用
texts.text:
A: Our company is piloting an AI assistant for drafting reports and emails. What do you think?
B: It's a double-edged sword — it boosts productivity, but there are real concerns about job displacement.
A: I think it's more about augmenting human work than replacing it, at least in the short term.
B: If AI handles sixty percent of a desk job's tasks, workforce restructuring seems inevitable.
A: Companies will need to invest in retraining programs to prepare workers for new roles.
B: The technology isn't the problem — how we manage the transition is what matters.

texts.title: Social Media and Teen Mental Health
texts.titleJa: SNSと若者のメンタルヘルス
texts.text:
A: A new study found teens spending over three hours daily on social media are far more likely to experience anxiety.
B: That's concerning. Though I wonder how much is correlation versus causation.
A: There's probably truth in both directions. Either way, the data is hard to ignore.
B: Some platforms are introducing screen time limits and removing harmful content more proactively.
A: It's progress, but self-regulation only goes so far. There may need to be legislation.
B: Especially for minors — age verification and parental controls need to be far more robust.

texts.title: Hospital Ransomware Attack
texts.titleJa: 病院へのランサムウェア攻撃
texts.text: Cybersecurity officials have issued a high-level warning following a sophisticated ransomware attack that disrupted operations at several major hospitals across Europe. The attack encrypted critical patient data and demanded payment in cryptocurrency, forcing hospitals to cancel procedures and revert to manual record-keeping. Authorities suspect the attack was carried out by a criminal group with ties to state-sponsored actors. The incident has renewed calls for organizations to significantly upgrade their digital defenses. Experts recommend regular system audits, staff training on phishing attacks, and maintaining isolated backups of all critical data.

texts.title: Hybrid Work Policy
texts.titleJa: ハイブリッドワークの定着
texts.text:
A: Our company just announced a permanent hybrid policy — three days in the office, two from home.
B: I prefer it to a full return. I'm more productive at home for focused work, but value in-person collaboration.
A: I do worry about younger employees — they benefit from informal mentoring and being around experienced staff.
B: Some companies have introduced mandatory in-office days for new hires specifically for that reason.
A: The whole concept of the workplace has shifted permanently. We're not going back to the old model.
B: And I think that's broadly a good thing — as long as companies manage it thoughtfully.

texts.title: Return to the Moon
texts.titleJa: 月面有人着陸の再挑戦
texts.text:
A: NASA successfully landed a crew on the lunar surface for the first time in over fifty years.
B: The Artemis program has been years in the making. And this crew includes the first woman and first person of color to walk on the moon.
A: A historic moment on multiple levels. What's the long-term goal?
B: A permanent lunar base, then using it as a stepping stone for future missions to Mars.
A: The commercial space sector is accelerating development enormously.
B: Competition between public agencies and private companies is driving innovation faster than ever.

texts.title: Autonomous Taxi Experience
texts.titleJa: 自動運転タクシーに乗る
texts.text:
A: Self-driving taxis are now operating commercially in three major cities. Did you try one?
B: I did — it was slightly unnerving at first, but after a few minutes it felt remarkably smooth.
A: How did it handle complex situations — construction zones, unexpected pedestrians?
B: Better than I expected. It detected a cyclist emerging from between parked cars before I even noticed.
A: Though there are still significant regulatory and liability questions to resolve.
B: If there's an accident, who's responsible? The legal framework hasn't caught up with the technology yet.

texts.title: Cryptocurrency Debate
texts.titleJa: 暗号資産をめぐる議論
texts.text:
A: Bitcoin hit a new all-time high this week. Are you invested?
B: A small amount, yes — but I'm cautious. The volatility is extreme. It can lose forty percent in weeks.
A: The blockchain technology does seem to have real-world applications beyond currency.
B: Absolutely — supply chain tracking, digital identity, decentralized finance are all legitimate use cases.
A: But the environmental cost of mining is enormous. Some estimates compare Bitcoin's energy use to a mid-sized country.
B: The industry is under pressure to shift to more energy-efficient mechanisms. It's a real problem.

texts.title: 5G Transforms Healthcare
texts.titleJa: 5Gが変える医療
texts.text:
A: With 5G rolling out broadly, what do you think the biggest impact will be?
B: I think it's less about smartphones and more about enabling autonomous vehicles and remote surgery.
A: Remote surgery sounds futuristic.
B: It's already been demonstrated — surgeons operating on a patient in another country via robotic arms with near-zero latency.
A: That has profound implications for healthcare access in remote or underserved regions.
B: And for disaster response — expert medical assistance deployed remotely could save many lives.

texts.title: Smart City of the Future
texts.titleJa: スマートシティの未来
texts.text: Singapore has long been recognized as a global leader in smart city development. The city-state monitors traffic flow in real time using over a hundred thousand sensors, reducing average commute times by twenty-three percent compared to five years ago. Smart waste collection systems alert crews only when bins are full, cutting collection routes by a third. Water usage is tracked at the building level, enabling conservation measures that have reduced per capita consumption by fifteen percent. City planners note that the key to success has been not just the technology, but seamless integration of data across departments — and a government committed to long-term investment in digital infrastructure.

texts.title: Inflation and Cost of Living
texts.titleJa: インフレと生活コストの上昇
texts.text:
A: Inflation has really hit hard this year. My grocery bill is up almost thirty percent.
B: Same here. And rent in this city is becoming unaffordable for ordinary workers.
A: Central banks raised interest rates to control inflation, but that's made mortgages more expensive too.
B: It feels like a squeeze from all sides. Wages haven't kept pace with rising costs.
A: Economists are divided on whether this is temporary or a structural shift.
B: Either way, policymakers need to do more to protect lower-income households who are bearing the brunt.

texts.title: Supply Chain Disruption
texts.titleJa: サプライチェーンの混乱
texts.text:
A: Manufacturers are still struggling with supply chain disruptions years after the pandemic. Why so slow?
B: It's a combination — geopolitical tensions have reshuffled trade routes, and extreme weather keeps disrupting logistics.
A: And companies that over-relied on just-in-time inventory had no buffer when things went wrong.
B: Many are now diversifying suppliers and reshoring some production to build resilience.
A: That's the right direction, but it comes with higher costs that consumers ultimately absorb.
B: Globalization brought efficiency but also fragility. We're rethinking the balance.

texts.title: Stock Market Volatility
texts.titleJa: 株式市場の乱高下
texts.text:
A: The market dropped sharply this week on interest rate concerns. Are you worried?
B: Not particularly — I invest for the long term, so short-term volatility doesn't change my strategy.
A: That's wise. Though it's hard to stay calm when your portfolio drops ten percent in a week.
B: It is. But history shows markets recover. Panic selling is almost always a mistake.
A: What sectors look well-positioned going forward?
B: Clean energy, healthcare, and technology look strong despite near-term turbulence. Demographics strongly favor healthcare.

texts.title: Startup Culture Cooling Down
texts.titleJa: スタートアップブームの変調
texts.text:
A: Silicon Valley's startup scene is cooling after years of frenzied growth. What's driving that?
B: Rising interest rates made cheap capital disappear. Startups relying on endless funding without profitability are struggling.
A: The era of 'grow at any cost' seems over. Investors now want a clear path to revenue.
B: That's probably healthy long-term. Too many startups were overvalued on pure hype.
A: Asia's tech startup ecosystems are emerging strongly — particularly India and Southeast Asia.
B: Those markets have massive growth potential and increasingly sophisticated tech talent. The center of gravity is shifting.

texts.title: Free Trade vs. Protectionism
texts.titleJa: 自由貿易か保護主義か
texts.text:
A: The new regional trade agreement is expected to reduce tariffs across fifteen countries. Your take?
B: Free trade generally benefits economies overall, but gains aren't distributed evenly — some industries lose out.
A: Domestic manufacturers in protected sectors often face painful competition they're not prepared for.
B: The key is whether governments use the efficiency gains to fund retraining for displaced workers.
A: In theory, yes. In practice, those programs are often underfunded or poorly designed.
B: Trade policy is easy to criticize, but protectionism has significant costs of its own.

texts.title: Widening Wealth Gap
texts.titleJa: 格差の拡大
texts.text: A new IMF report highlights a widening gap between the world's wealthiest and the rest. The top one percent now controls over forty-five percent of global wealth — a proportion that has increased steadily for two decades. The pandemic accelerated inequality: lower-income service workers bore the brunt of job losses while asset prices in stocks and real estate soared, disproportionately benefiting those already wealthy. Economists warn that persistent inequality poses risks not only to social cohesion but to long-term growth, as it reduces consumer spending power. The report recommends progressive taxation, investment in public education, and stronger labor protections as key responses.

texts.title: Skills Mismatch in the Job Market
texts.titleJa: 雇用ミスマッチの問題
texts.text:
A: Unemployment is at historic lows, yet companies say they can't find qualified workers. What's going on?
B: It's what economists call a skills mismatch. Available jobs require different skills than many workers currently have.
A: Technology is disrupting roles faster than education systems can adapt.
B: A manufacturing worker whose job has been automated can't easily retrain as a software engineer in six months.
A: Some companies are investing in internal upskilling programs. Amazon pledged to retrain a hundred thousand workers.
B: Governments need to subsidize similar programs broadly. It's cheaper than dealing with long-term unemployment.

texts.title: Over-Tourism Problem
texts.titleJa: オーバーツーリズムの課題
texts.text:
A: International tourism has recovered to pre-pandemic levels. But I hear destinations are struggling.
B: Places like Kyoto, Venice, and Barcelona are essentially being loved to death. The local culture suffers.
A: Some cities are introducing visitor caps, higher entry fees, or zoning restrictions.
B: It's a delicate balance — tourism drives jobs and revenue, but you can undermine what makes a place worth visiting.
A: There's also the environmental impact — cruise ships and air travel have enormous carbon footprints.
B: Sustainable tourism needs to become the norm, not the exception. The industry has to change its model.

4001.meaning: 法的拘束力のある約束
4001.note: 国際条約・合意でよく使われる表現。

4001.meaning: 執行メカニズム・強制力

4002.note: ニュース英語の定番表現。

4002.meaning: ペースを加速しなければならない

4003.meaning: 焼け石に水・ほんのわずかな貢献
4003.note: 「大きな問題に対してほんの少ししか効果がない」という比喩的表現。

4003.meaning: 使い捨てプラスチック
4003.note: 環境問題の文脈で最頻出の語句。

4003.meaning: 根本から・発生源から
4003.note: 問題の「根本解決」を表す際に使う表現。

4003.meaning: 小さな行動も積み重なれば大きくなる
4003.note: 環境・社会問題の議論でよく使われる表現。

4004.reading: アップフロントコスト
4004.meaning: 初期費用・先行コスト
4004.note: 製品購入やプロジェクト開始時にかかる費用を指す。
EV普及政策の文脈で頻出の語。
EV普及の課題として常に挙げられるキーワード。

4004.reading: ファストチャージャー

4004.note: 「次の買い替えのタイミングで」という口語的表現。

4005.reading: カーボンニュートラリティ
4005.meaning: カーボンニュートラル・炭素中立
4005.note: CO₂排出量と吸収量を均衡させた状態。
phase out は「段階的に廃止する」という重要な動詞句。

4005.meaning: 再び議題に上がる・復活する
4005.note: 議論や選択肢として再浮上する、という慣用表現。

4005.meaning: ベースロード電力（常時安定供給が必要な電力）
4005.note: 電力供給の文脈で使われる技術用語。

4005.meaning: 分野横断的な政策調整

4006.note: 「昼夜問わず働き続ける」という慣用表現。

4006.meaning: 避難を余儀なくされた・住む場所を失った
4006.note: 災害・紛争報道で非常に多く使われる語。

4006.meaning: 緊急に必要とされている
4006.note: 物資・支援が切実に求められている状況を表す。

4007.reading: マリン・プロテクテッド・エリア
4007.note: 略称MPA。国際的な海洋保全の枠組みで使われる用語。

4007.reading: オーバーフィッシング
4007.note: 海洋生態系破壊の主要原因の一つ。
新たな環境リスクとして注目されているトピック。

4007.meaning: 密接に連携する・両立して進める
4007.note: 「二つのことが切り離せない関係にある」という慣用表現。

4008.reading: アーバン・ヒートアイランド
4008.meaning: 都市ヒートアイランド現象
4008.note: 都市部が周囲より気温が高くなる現象。
都市内の連続した緑地ネットワークを指す。

4008.meaning: 測定できるほど・明らかに
4008.note: 改善効果が数値として確認できる程度であることを強調する副詞。

4008.meaning: 住みやすい・暮らしやすい
4008.note: 都市計画・まちづくりの文脈で頻出の語。

4008.meaning: 人間の幸福・福祉を優先する

4009.reading: ダブルエッジドソード
4009.note: ビジネス議論で頻出の比喩表現。

4009.meaning: 人間の仕事を補完する
4009.note: AI議論の重要キーワード。

4010.meaning: 相関関係と因果関係の違い
4010.note: 「相関があっても因果関係とは限らない」という統計・科学的議論の基本概念。

4010.reading: スクリーンタイムリミット
4010.meaning: スクリーンタイム制限

4010.reading: セルフレギュレーション
4010.note: 企業や業界が法規制なしに自ら設ける規制を指す。

4010.reading: エイジベリフィケーション
4010.note: 未成年保護の観点からSNS規制の文脈で重要なキーワード。

4010.reading: ペアレンタルコントロール
4010.meaning: 保護者による使用制限
4010.note: 子どものデジタル利用を管理するための機能・設定。

4011.meaning: 身代金型サイバー攻撃
国家支援を受けた（ハッカー）集団

4012.meaning: 恒久的なハイブリッド勤務制度

4012.reading: インフォーマルメンタリング
4012.meaning: 非公式な指導・日常的なメンタリング
4012.note: 制度的なトレーニングではなく、日常業務の中で自然に行われる指導のこと。

4012.reading: インパーソンコラボレーション
4012.note: テレワークと対比して使われる。

4012.meaning: 思慮深く管理する・丁寧に運営する

4012.note: コロナ後の働き方が元に戻らないことを表す表現。

4013.reading: ステッピングストーン
4013.note: 次の大きな目標に向けた中間地点を比喩的に表す表現。

4013.meaning: 長年にわたって準備されてきた
4013.note: 「何年もかけて実現した」という意味の慣用表現。

4013.reading: コマーシャルスペースセクター
4013.note: SpaceXなどの民間企業による宇宙開発分野を指す。

4013.meaning: イノベーションを促進する

4014.reading: セルフドライビングタクシー

4014.note: 自動運転普及における法的課題を指す。

4014.meaning: 不安な・神経を逆なでする
4014.note: 新技術に初めて触れたときの心理的不安感を表す形容詞。

4014.phrase: the legal framework hasn't caught up
4014.meaning: 法的な枠組みが技術に追いついていない
4014.note: 技術と法律の乖離を表すよく使われるフレーズ。

4015.note: 株・暗号資産・記録の文脈で頻出の語句。

4015.reading: ディセントラライズドファイナンス
4015.note: 略称DeFi。ブロックチェーンを利用した金融サービス全般。

4015.meaning: サプライチェーン追跡
4015.note: ブロックチェーンの実用例として頻繁に引用される応用分野。

4015.meaning: エネルギー効率の高い仕組み
4015.note: Proof of Stakeなどへの移行を指す。

4015.reading: エクストリームボラティリティ
4015.note: 暗号資産のリスクを表す際の定番表現。

4016.meaning: 広範囲に展開・普及する
4016.note: roll out は新技術やサービスを段階的に広める際に使う動詞句。

4016.reading: リモートサージェリー
4016.note: 5Gの低遅延を活用した医療応用の代表例。

4016.reading: ニアゼロレイテンシー
4016.note: 5Gの技術的特徴を表すキーワード。latencyは「通信遅延」。

4016.meaning: 十分なサービスが届いていない地域
4016.note: 医療・教育・インフラ格差の文脈で使われる表現。

4016.reading: ロボティックアームズ

4017.note: 遅延なくデータを処理・表示することを指す。

4017.reading: パーキャピタコンサンプション
4017.note: 統計・政策文書で頻出の表現。

4017.reading: シームレスインテグレーション
4017.meaning: シームレスな統合・円滑な連携
4017.note: スマートシティ成功の鍵として言及されることが多い。

4017.meaning: デジタルインフラ・情報通信基盤

4018.note: 「最も大きな打撃を受ける」という意味のイディオム。

4019.reading: ジャストインタイムインベントリ
4019.meaning: ジャストインタイム在庫管理
4019.note: 必要な時に必要な量だけ調達する在庫方式。効率的だが在庫バッファがなく脆弱。

4019.reading: サプライチェーンディスラプション
4019.meaning: サプライチェーンの混乱・寸断
国内回帰（生産拠点を自国に戻すこと）

4019.note: サプライチェーンのリスク管理策として重要な概念。

4019.phrase: globalization brought efficiency but also fragility
4019.meaning: グローバル化は効率をもたらしたが脆弱性も生んだ
4019.note: グローバルサプライチェーンのトレードオフを端的に表す表現。

4020.note: 株式市場の下落要因としてよく報じられる表現。

4020.meaning: 狼狽売り・パニック売り
4020.note: 相場下落時に感情的に株を売る行動を指す。
turbulenceは「乱気流」が原義。金融市場の不安定な状態を比喩的に表す。

4020.meaning: 人口動態が強く後押しする
4020.note: 高齢化社会が医療セクターへの投資を有利にするという文脈。

4021.reading: フレンジードグロース
4021.meaning: 熱狂的な成長・狂乱の成長
4021.note: frenzy（熱狂・狂乱）から派生。過度な盛り上がりを表す。

4021.meaning: 低コストの資金・安い資本
4021.note: 低金利時代に大量に調達できた資金を指す。
投資家がスタートアップに求める収益実現の見通しを指す。

4021.meaning: 純粋な誇大宣伝だけで過大評価された
4021.note: hypeは「過剰な宣伝・誇大広告」。

4021.meaning: 重心が移動している・中心地が変わりつつある
4021.note: テクノロジーや経済の主導権の地理的変化を表す比喩表現。

4022.reading: リージョナルトレードアグリーメント
4022.note: 略称RTA。複数国間の関税削減・貿易促進の協定。
貿易政策の最重要キーワードの一つ。

4022.meaning: 失業を余儀なくされた労働者
4022.note: 貿易自由化や技術革新による雇用喪失を指す文脈で使われる。

4022.meaning: 効率化による利益・効率向上の恩恵
4022.note: 自由貿易の経済的メリットを指す。

4022.meaning: 保護主義にも大きなコストがある
4022.note: 自由貿易対保護主義の議論で必ず言及されるポイント。

4023.meaning: 不均衡に恩恵を受ける

4024.meaning: スキルのミスマッチ・技能のずれ
4024.note: 求職者のスキルと求人の要件が一致しない現象を指す経済学用語。

4024.meaning: 職種・仕事を破壊・変革する
4024.note: disruptは「根本的に覆す」という意味。テクノロジーが雇用を変える文脈で頻出。

4024.reading: インターナルアップスキリングプログラム
4024.meaning: 社内スキルアップ研修制度
4024.note: upskill は「既存従業員に新たなスキルを習得させる」という動詞。

4024.meaning: 補助金を出す・助成する
4024.note: 政府が費用を負担して支援することを指す。

4024.reading: ロングタームアンエンプロイメント
4024.note: 個人と社会への深刻なコストとして経済政策議論で重要視される。

4025.meaning: 過剰な訪問者で台無しにされる
4025.note: オーバーツーリズムを表す慣用表現。
